label: State Senator
subject: S447, H3155, and H4013 need a hearing and they need amendments
body:
Dear [NAME],

Last year, two Greenville sisters driving a rental car were pulled over at gunpoint when a Flock Safety camera wrongly flagged their vehicle as stolen. Police drew weapons. Both women were handcuffed. The car wasn't stolen. They're now suing Greenville PD.

More than 1,000 Flock Safety cameras are deployed across South Carolina by 110+ law enforcement agencies. SLED's statewide database has logged 422 million license plate reads with 3-year retention. Flock secretly gave federal agencies direct access to local police cameras across the country without telling those agencies. Their CEO denied it on camera in July 2025 and admitted it three weeks later.

Three bills are waiting for a hearing: S447, H3155, and H4013. All three are stalled in committee. They need to move, but they also need to be stronger. As written, all three regulate what government agencies can do with camera data. None of them touch what Flock itself does with it.

Flock operates the network. Flock holds the data on its own servers. Flock decides which agencies can query which cameras. A 90-day retention limit for police means nothing if Flock keeps the data indefinitely. A ban on government selling data means nothing if cross-jurisdiction search is a product feature Flock sells. Once data enters the network, local police cannot pull it back.

Any bill that passes must be amended to:
- Require that private vendors like Flock delete data on the same schedule as law enforcement
- Give local jurisdictions the right to block outside agencies from querying their camera data through the vendor's network
- Prohibit vendors from granting federal access without explicit local authorization
- Require public disclosure of all data-sharing agreements between the vendor and any government entity

Without these provisions, the bills regulate the buyers but leave the seller unaccountable. Please push for a committee hearing and fight for these amendments.

Sincerely,
[Your name and address]

label: Greenville City Council
subject: Greenville needs a public vote on Flock Safety
body:
Dear [NAME],

Last year, two Greenville sisters driving a rental car were pulled over at gunpoint after a Flock Safety camera wrongly flagged their vehicle as stolen. Police drew weapons. Both women were handcuffed. The car wasn't stolen. They're now suing Greenville PD.

Greenville PD runs 57 Flock Safety cameras. The first went up in 2021, paid for with civil asset forfeiture funds. There was no council vote on oversight, no rules about who can see the data, how long it's kept, or who checks how it's used. Flock secretly gave federal agencies direct access to local police cameras across the country without telling those agencies. Their CEO denied it on camera and admitted it three weeks later.

Once Greenville's data is in that network, the city can't pull it back. That's how the system is built. None of it requires a warrant. The city's camera contracts are also designed to pull in private HOA cameras and residents' Ring cameras, growing the network beyond what's publicly visible with no additional vote.

Three state bills (S447, H3155, H4013) would set rules for police use of camera data, but none of them regulate what Flock itself does with the data on its own servers. Until the state closes that gap, local action is the only check.

I'm asking you to:
- Hold a public hearing on Greenville's Flock Safety contract and how the data is actually shared
- Pass an ordinance requiring council approval before any new surveillance technology is deployed
- Refuse to expand the system until residents have had a real say

Sedona, AZ; Hays County, TX; and Cambridge, MA all looked closely at how Flock works and decided it wasn't worth it. Greenville residents deserve the same chance to weigh in.

Sincerely,
[Your name and address]

The Sheriff's Office has no county-level oversight policy for how this data is accessed, shared, or audited. The county's SafeWatch program is pulling HOA and private cameras into the same network, expanding surveillance without any public vote.

Flock's CEO denied federal data sharing on camera in July 2025. He admitted it three weeks later. Once location data enters the network, no local policy can pull it back. Federal agencies don't need a warrant to search it.

Three state bills (S447, H3155, H4013) would set rules for police use of camera data, but none regulate what Flock does with data on its own servers. Until the state closes that gap, county oversight is the only check.

Last year in Greenville, two sisters were handcuffed at gunpoint after a Flock camera wrongly flagged their rental car as stolen. They're suing. That's what unregulated surveillance looks like in practice.

I'm asking the County Council to:
- Require the Sheriff's Office to disclose all Flock camera locations and data-sharing agreements
- Establish a county oversight policy covering data retention, access, and auditing
- Hold a public hearing before any expansion of the camera network

Hays County, TX and Sedona, AZ cancelled their Flock contracts after reviewing how the data sharing works. Greenville County residents deserve the same transparency.

Sincerely,
[Your name and address]

Sincerely,
[Your name and address]

label: Richland County Council
subject: The Richland County Sheriff quoted $9,000 to hide two CSV files from the public
body:
Dear [NAME],

In December 2025, a journalist asked the Richland County Sheriff's Department for two CSV files showing how the department uses its Flock Safety cameras and which outside agencies access the data. The response: a bill for $9,152, a six-week timeline, and a demand for 25% upfront. Florence PD handed over the same records for free in one day. Bluffton PD mailed a USB drive for $20.

This isn't new. The Richland County Sheriff's Department was ordered to pay $6,000 in FOIA penalties in 2020 and previously ignored a FOIA audit request entirely. The department operates 5 Flock cameras. Columbia PD runs its own ALPR system through the SLED statewide database. And SLED itself, headquartered right here in Richland County, stores 422 million license plate reads with 3-year retention, accessible to 2,000+ users across 99+ agencies.

Flock Safety's CEO was asked on camera in July 2025 whether his company had federal contracts. He said no. Three weeks later he admitted Flock had secretly given Customs and Border Protection direct access to local police cameras without telling the departments involved. Once data enters the Flock network, local agencies cannot pull it back.

Three state bills (S447, H3155, H4013) would set rules for police use of camera data, but all three are stalled in committee and none regulate what Flock does with data on its servers. Until the state acts, county oversight is the only safeguard, and right now Richland County has none.

I am asking the County Council to:
- Require the Sheriff's Department to disclose all Flock camera locations and data-sharing agreements at no cost to the public
- Establish a county oversight policy covering data retention, access controls, and regular auditing of ALPR use
- Require council approval before any expansion of automated surveillance technology

Denver, CO passed a surveillance oversight ordinance requiring public notice and city council approval before new systems go live. Richland County, sitting at the center of the state's ALPR infrastructure, should lead instead of stonewalling the public for $9,000.

Sincerely,
[Your name and address]

label: Columbia City Council
subject: Columbia's Flock cameras are already failing the transparency test
body:
Dear [NAME],

In October 2024, Columbia City Council voted 5-2 to approve 130 Flock Safety cameras at $539,400 in the first year and $476,000 annually after that. The council also unanimously approved a new oversight process for surveillance technology. That was the right call. But the oversight is already failing.

Columbia PD's Flock transparency portal does not show which police beats or wards have cameras. The department has not met the disclosure requirements the council established. Meanwhile, the cameras are live, data is flowing to SLED's 422-million-read statewide database, and Flock's national network remains open to outside agencies.

Flock's CEO denied having federal contracts on camera in July 2025 and admitted it weeks later. The company had secretly given Customs and Border Protection access to local police cameras across the country without telling the departments involved. Columbia's 90-day data retention policy means nothing if Flock keeps the data longer on its own servers.

The council approved this technology with guardrails. Those guardrails aren't working. That's fixable, but only if the council acts.

I am asking you to:
- Require Columbia PD to bring its Flock transparency portal into full compliance with the oversight ordinance, including camera locations by beat and ward
- Amend the Flock contract to explicitly prohibit federal agency access without city council authorization and ban use for immigration enforcement
- Conduct a public audit of how camera data has been accessed and shared in the first year of deployment

Austin, TX terminated its Flock contract after reviewing data-sharing concerns. Columbia doesn't need to go that far, but the council must enforce the oversight it already promised.

Sincerely,
[Your name and address]

label: Charleston County Council
subject: A Charleston County deputy used surveillance cameras to stalk his wife
body:
Dear [NAME],

In June 2024, North Charleston Police Lt. Ryan Terrell admitted to using city-owned surveillance cameras to track his wife, whom he suspected of having an affair with a fellow officer. Data logs showed Terrell accessed camera feeds on multiple days, following her movements via live video until he confronted her in a Target parking lot. He was demoted but not fired.

That's what happens when surveillance infrastructure exists without real oversight. And the infrastructure in Charleston County is large. The Sheriff's Office operates 25+ Flock cameras and expanded coverage to James Island in May 2024. Charleston PD runs 12 Flock cameras. Mount Pleasant PD participates in the SLED statewide ALPR database. The Charleston County SO also operates DataWorks Plus facial recognition technology, with crowd-scanning capabilities deployed since 2013.

All of this data feeds into networks where Flock secretly gave federal agencies direct access without telling local departments. Their CEO denied it publicly and admitted it weeks later. SLED retains 422 million plate reads for three years.

Three state bills (S447, H3155, H4013) would set rules for police use of camera data, but none regulate what Flock does with data on its servers. Until the state acts, county oversight is the only check.

I am asking the County Council to:
- Require disclosure of all ALPR and facial recognition deployments, costs, and data-sharing agreements across county agencies
- Establish a county surveillance oversight policy with mandatory audit trails and penalties for misuse
- Hold a public hearing before any renewal or expansion of surveillance technology contracts

Santa Cruz, CA banned government use of facial recognition and established strict oversight of all surveillance technology. Charleston County has facial recognition and ALPR systems running with no comparable safeguards.

Sincerely,
[Your name and address]

label: North Charleston City Council
subject: Lt. Terrell proved why $2.5M in surveillance needs real oversight
body:
Dear [NAME],

In June 2024, North Charleston Police Lt. Ryan Terrell, the officer who oversaw the city's technology unit including its Verkada camera system, admitted to using city-owned surveillance cameras to track his wife's movements on multiple days. He followed her via live video and confronted her in a Target parking lot. He was demoted to patrol and given six months probation. He was not fired.

North Charleston has invested $2.5 million in 800+ Verkada surveillance cameras, 34 Rekor ALPR units, a Fusus real-time crime center that can pull feeds from private cameras, and a Joint Operations Center at City Hall. The council approved an additional $1.5 million for the Joint Operations Center buildout in 2025. Annual operating costs will reach $2.2 million after year five.

This is the largest municipal surveillance system in the state, and an officer already abused it. The system was built to integrate private cameras from businesses and residents, expanding its reach far beyond public cameras alone. There is no public oversight framework for who accesses this data, how it is audited, or what happens when someone misuses it beyond a demotion.

Flock Safety's CEO denied federal contracts in July 2025 and admitted weeks later that the company had given Border Patrol access to local cameras nationwide. North Charleston's ALPR data feeds into SLED's 422-million-read statewide database.

I am asking the City Council to:
- Establish a mandatory audit and access log system for all surveillance cameras, with annual public reporting
- Create clear penalties for surveillance misuse that go beyond demotion
- Require council approval before adding private cameras to the city's Fusus network or expanding the ALPR system

Eugene, OR rejected a Flock proposal after residents organized against unregulated surveillance. North Charleston has already built the system. The least the council can do is build the rules.

Sincerely,
[Your name and address]

Their CEO denied it on camera in July 2025 and admitted it three weeks later.

Myrtle Beach PD claims a 47% decrease in Part 1 crime since 2015 and credits the cameras. But a National Institute of Justice study of Detroit's similar Project Green Light found no evidence that cameras reduced violent crime. Meanwhile, millions of tourists visiting the Grand Strand each year have their plate data captured and stored without any knowledge or consent.

Three state bills (S447, H3155, H4013) are stalled in committee. None regulate what Flock does with the data on its own servers.

I am asking the County Council to:
- Conduct a county-wide inventory of all ALPR deployments, costs, and data-sharing agreements across every agency in Horry County
- Establish a county surveillance oversight policy with data retention standards and mandatory auditing
- Require council approval before any agency expands its ALPR network or enters new data-sharing agreements

Hays County, TX cancelled its Flock contract after reviewing how data sharing works. Horry County has more cameras than most Texas counties ever considered, and zero oversight.

Sincerely,
[Your name and address]

label: Myrtle Beach City Council
subject: 38.7 million plate reads a year deserves more than a press release
body:
Dear [NAME],

Myrtle Beach PD's Real Time Crime Center recorded 38.7 million license plate reads in 2019 alone. The system now provides over 1,900 camera views. The FY 2025-2026 budget allocates $300,000 for camera repairs and new phases, and 118 new camera views were added around The Market Common in 2024 with 250 more planned for 2025. The department also runs ShotSpotter at $90,000 per year and has a Ring doorbell partnership giving police access to private residential footage.

This is one of the most extensive municipal surveillance systems in South Carolina. Yet there is no public transparency portal, no city council oversight ordinance, and no published policy on who can access the ALPR data or how long it's kept.

Flock Safety gave federal agencies direct access to local cameras nationwide without telling departments. SLED stores 422 million plate reads for three years. Millions of tourists visit Myrtle Beach each year. Their plate data is captured, stored, and searchable by agencies they've never heard of.

The department claims a 47% drop in Part 1 crime since 2015 and credits the cameras. Independent research, including a National Institute of Justice study of a comparable system in Detroit, found no evidence that cameras reduce violent crime.

I am asking the City Council to:
- Commission an independent audit of the Real Time Crime Center's data collection, retention, and sharing practices
- Pass an ordinance requiring council approval before expanding surveillance technology or adding private cameras to the city network
- Establish a public transparency portal with camera locations, access policies, and annual reporting

Austin, TX terminated its Flock contract after reviewing privacy concerns. Myrtle Beach has a larger system than most cities that have taken action. The council should make sure residents and visitors know how their data is being used.

Sincerely,
[Your name and address]

label: Conway City Council
subject: 54 cameras for 25,000 people deserves a public conversation
body:
Dear [NAME],

Conway PD operates 54 Flock Safety cameras as of September 2025. For a city of about 25,000, that's roughly one camera for every 460 residents. The department also recently installed ShotSpotter gunshot detection. There has been no public hearing, no city council vote on oversight, and no published policy for how the ALPR data is collected, stored, or shared.

That data does not stay in Conway. It flows into SLED's statewide database: 422 million plate reads, 3-year retention, accessible to 2,000+ users across 99+ agencies. Flock Safety gave federal agencies direct access to local cameras without telling the departments involved. Their CEO denied it on camera in July 2025 and admitted it weeks later.

Conway's 54 cameras sit within a broader Horry County surveillance corridor. Myrtle Beach PD logged 38.7 million plate reads in a single year. North Myrtle Beach is building a $2 million Real Time Crime Center. Anyone driving between Conway and the coast passes through multiple overlapping ALPR zones.

Three state bills (S447, H3155, H4013) would set rules for police use of camera data, but all are stalled and none regulate Flock's own data practices.

I am asking the City Council to:
- Hold a public hearing on Conway PD's Flock camera deployment, including camera locations, costs, and data-sharing agreements
- Pass an ordinance requiring city council approval before deploying or expanding automated surveillance systems
- Establish a data retention limit shorter than SLED's 3-year default and publish a use policy

Staunton, VA cancelled its Flock contract after residents raised questions about data sharing. Conway residents haven't even had the chance to ask.

Sincerely,
[Your name and address]

label: Florence County Council
subject: Sheriff Joye is putting cameras on private property to dodge state rules
body:
Dear [NAME],

When SCDOT paused permits for Flock cameras on state road rights-of-way in 2024, Florence County Sheriff T.J. Joye found a workaround: installing cameras on private property next to public roads. Homeowners volunteered their land, and the sheriff told reporters they just want to help catch criminals. He had 76 Flock cameras in patrol cars and 14 stationary units he wanted to deploy.

This tactic gets around a state regulatory pause that exists because SCDOT's own Secretary discovered over 200 unpermitted Flock cameras on state roads and raised unresolved privacy concerns. The Florence County legislative delegation even introduced H5146 to force SCDOT to cooperate with the county's camera installations.

Meanwhile, the Sheriff's Office retains ALPR data for one year, while Florence PD deletes after 30 days. Same county, two agencies, two different retention policies, because there are no standards. All of this data feeds into SLED's 422-million-read database and Flock's national network, where the CEO lied about giving federal agencies access.

Three state bills (S447, H3155, H4013) are stalled. None regulate what Flock does with data on its servers.

I am asking the County Council to:
- Require the Sheriff's Office to disclose all camera locations, including those on private property, and all data-sharing agreements
- Establish a uniform county data retention standard for all agencies operating ALPR cameras
- Require council approval before any further expansion of surveillance camera deployments, including the private property workaround

Sedona, AZ and Hays County, TX both cancelled Flock contracts after examining how the data actually flows. Florence County is expanding faster than almost any county in the state. The council should know what it's approving.

Sincerely,
[Your name and address]

label: Florence City Council
subject: A $400,000 state grant bought 30 cameras and no oversight
body:
Dear [NAME],

Florence PD's 30 Flock Safety cameras were funded by a $397,500 state legislative appropriation secured by Sen. Mike Reichenbach, not from the city budget. The cameras cover most gateways in and out of the city, including the Highway 76 corridor. The data auto-deletes after 30 days from Florence PD's system. But it does not auto-delete from SLED's statewide database, which stores 422 million plate reads for three years, or from Flock's national network.

Flock Safety gave federal agencies direct access to local cameras nationwide without telling departments. Their CEO denied it in July 2025 and admitted it weeks later. Florence PD's 30-day retention means nothing if the data lives elsewhere for years.

The Florence County Sheriff's Office operates an additional 14 stationary cameras and 76 mobile units, with data retained for one year. The sheriff is installing cameras on private property to bypass SCDOT's permit pause. Florence is building a surveillance grid from both city and county directions with no coordinated oversight.

Three state bills (S447, H3155, H4013) are stalled. None regulate Flock's own data handling.

I am asking the City Council to:
- Amend Florence PD's Flock contract to explicitly prohibit federal agency access without city council authorization
- Require annual public reporting on camera locations, alert types, data-sharing partners, and access logs
- Hold a public hearing on whether the current 30-camera deployment should expand, contract, or stay the same

Cambridge, MA adopted strict transparency requirements and access controls for surveillance technology. Florence's cameras were funded by a state grant with no strings attached. The city council should attach some.

Sincerely,
[Your name and address]

label: Beaufort County Council
subject: 83 million plate scans in one year, and the sheriff says he can track you across the country
body:
Dear [NAME],

In its first year of operation, the Beaufort County Sheriff's ALPR cameras captured 83 million license plate images. Officers conducted 4,320 individual searches, about 11 per day. Sheriff PJ Tanner told the Island Packet: "For the most part, we can track you all over the country."

Beaufort County has invested over $600,000 in nearly 70 ALPR cameras, using both Rekor and Flock Safety vendors at the same time. The Sheriff's Office chose Rekor; Beaufort PD, Bluffton PD, and Hilton Head use Flock. Councilman David Bartholomew was the sole dissenter on the ARPA funding request, asking what this technology would be used for 10 or 15 years from now. He said he could not support spending federal relief funds on surveillance without state regulation.

Councilman Bartholomew was right to ask. Flock Safety gave federal agencies direct access to local cameras without telling departments. SLED stores 422 million plate reads for three years. Hilton Head Island alone draws millions of visitors whose plate data is captured without their knowledge.

Three state bills (S447, H3155, H4013) are stalled. None regulate vendor data practices.

I am asking the County Council to:
- Require the Sheriff's Office and all municipal agencies to publish unified data-sharing agreements and access policies
- Establish a county surveillance oversight committee with civilian representation to review ALPR use annually
- Adopt a county ordinance requiring council approval before expanding surveillance technology or entering new vendor contracts

Santa Cruz, CA banned government facial recognition and imposed strict surveillance oversight. Beaufort County is running two separate vendor systems that together scan 83 million plates a year. That scale demands accountability.

Sincerely,
[Your name and address]

label: Beaufort City Council
subject: Beaufort PD's Flock cameras feed a network the city does not control
body:
Dear [NAME],

Beaufort PD operates Flock Safety license plate reader cameras. The data feeds into Flock's national network and SLED's statewide database, which holds 422 million plate reads with 3-year retention, accessible to 2,000+ users. The Beaufort County Sheriff's Office runs a separate system using Rekor cameras that captured 83 million plate images in its first year. Between the city and county systems, Beaufort residents are tracked by two competing vendor networks with different data handling practices.

Flock Safety's CEO denied federal contracts on camera in July 2025 and admitted it weeks later. The company had given Customs and Border Protection direct access to local cameras nationwide without telling departments. Beaufort PD cannot control who accesses data once it enters the Flock network.

Sheriff Tanner has publicly stated he can track vehicles across the country using these partnerships. That's a mass surveillance network operating without local rules.

Three state bills (S447, H3155, H4013) are stalled. None regulate what vendors do with data on their servers.

I am asking the City Council to:
- Require Beaufort PD to publish a transparency portal showing camera locations, data-sharing partners, and access logs
- Pass an ordinance setting data retention limits and requiring council approval before expanding surveillance technology
- Coordinate with the county to establish unified oversight standards across all agencies operating ALPR cameras in Beaufort

Eugene, OR rejected a Flock proposal after residents organized against the lack of oversight. Beaufort already has cameras deployed. The city should at least build the oversight framework that Eugene demanded before deployment.

Sincerely,
[Your name and address]

label: Hilton Head Island Town Council
subject: Millions of visitors' plate data is being captured with no town oversight
body:
Dear [NAME],

Hilton Head Island uses Flock Safety license plate reader cameras. The Beaufort County Sheriff's Office operates a separate system with Rekor cameras that captured 83 million plate images in its first year. Sheriff PJ Tanner has publicly stated he can track vehicles across the country. Hilton Head draws millions of visitors annually, and every one of their license plates is being scanned, stored, and made searchable across a national network.

There is no town oversight framework. No published policy on data retention, access, or sharing. No public transparency portal. No town council vote authorizing the deployment or setting rules for how tourist and resident data is handled.

Flock Safety gave federal agencies direct access to local cameras without telling departments. Their CEO lied about it publicly. SLED retains 422 million plate reads for three years. For a tourism economy, the reputational risk of unregulated mass surveillance should concern every elected official.

Three state bills (S447, H3155, H4013) would set some rules but are stalled and do not regulate vendor data practices.

I am asking the Town Council to:
- Require a full accounting of all ALPR cameras operating on Hilton Head Island, their costs, and data-sharing agreements
- Pass a town ordinance requiring council approval and public notice before any surveillance technology is deployed or expanded on the island
- Establish a data retention limit and publish a clear policy governing access to visitor and resident plate data

Folly Beach, just up the coast, operates one of the few public Flock transparency portals in the state, with published policies and a 30-day retention limit. If a small beach town can manage transparency, Hilton Head can too.

Sincerely,
[Your name and address]

label: York County Council
subject: 58+ cameras across six agencies and no county-level oversight
body:
Dear [NAME],

York County has at least 58 Flock Safety cameras spread across the County Sheriff's Office, Rock Hill PD, Fort Mill PD, Tega Cay PD, Clover PD, and the Catawba Nation. Tega Cay has gone so far as to create a "virtual gate," cameras positioned to log every vehicle entering and leaving the city. Six agencies, no coordinated oversight, and no public discussion about who can access the combined data.

All of this feeds into SLED's statewide database: 422 million plate reads, 3-year retention, 2,000+ users across 99+ agencies. Flock Safety gave federal agencies direct access to local cameras without telling departments. Their CEO denied it in July 2025 and admitted it weeks later. Once data enters the network, no local agency can control who searches it.

York County's proximity to Charlotte means residents regularly cross state lines. The ALPR data collected here doesn't respect jurisdictional boundaries. It flows into national networks that link South Carolina cameras to systems in North Carolina and beyond.

Three state bills (S447, H3155, H4013) are stalled. None regulate Flock's own data practices.

I am asking the County Council to:
- Conduct a county-wide inventory of ALPR deployments across all six agencies, including camera locations, costs, and data-sharing agreements
- Establish a county oversight policy with unified data retention standards and mandatory auditing
- Require council approval before any agency in the county expands its surveillance camera network

Denver, CO requires public notice and city council approval before new surveillance systems are deployed. York County has six agencies operating independently with no comparable safeguard.

Sincerely,
[Your name and address]

label: Rock Hill City Council
subject: Rock Hill needs oversight before surveillance cameras expand further
body:
Dear [NAME],

Rock Hill PD operates Flock Safety license plate reader cameras as part of a York County network that includes at least 58 cameras across six agencies. Tega Cay has created a "virtual gate" logging every vehicle that enters or leaves the city. Rock Hill residents driving through the county are captured by multiple overlapping systems with no unified rules.

All of this data flows into SLED's statewide database: 422 million plate reads, 3-year retention, accessible to 2,000+ users. Flock Safety gave federal agencies direct access to local cameras without telling departments. Their CEO denied it publicly and admitted it weeks later. Once the data enters the network, Rock Hill PD cannot control who searches it.

Rock Hill's location near the North Carolina border means cross-state commuters have their plate data captured daily and stored in systems that link South Carolina and North Carolina surveillance networks. There is no public transparency portal, no city council oversight policy, and no published rules about data retention or access.

Three state bills (S447, H3155, H4013) are stalled in committee. None regulate what Flock does with data on its servers.

I am asking the City Council to:
- Require Rock Hill PD to disclose all camera locations, costs, and data-sharing agreements
- Pass an ordinance requiring city council approval before deploying or expanding automated surveillance technology
- Establish a public use policy with data retention limits and mandatory access logging

Staunton, VA cancelled its Flock contract after residents raised concerns about unregulated data sharing. Rock Hill residents deserve a say before the network grows further.

Sincerely,
[Your name and address]

label: Newberry County Council
subject: 40 Flock cameras for 38,000 residents, and no public debate
body:
Dear [NAME],

The Newberry County Sheriff's Office has 40 Flock Safety license plate reader cameras. That is one of the highest per-capita ALPR rates in South Carolina for a county of about 38,000 people. Nobody held a public hearing. The county council never voted on oversight rules. There is no portal showing where the cameras are or who can pull the data.

Every one of those 40 cameras feeds into SLED's statewide database, which holds 422 million plate reads, keeps them for three years, and gives access to over 2,000 users at 99+ agencies. Flock Safety also quietly gave federal agencies direct access to local police cameras across the country. When confronted about it in July 2025, their CEO denied it on camera. Three weeks later he admitted it was true. Once Newberry's data enters that network, no one locally can control who searches it.

This has already gone wrong elsewhere. In Greenville, two sisters were handcuffed at gunpoint after a Flock camera wrongly flagged their rental car as stolen. In Spartanburg, the county sheriff who oversaw surveillance deployments pled guilty to federal criminal charges. Hundreds of cameras across the Upstate and Midlands operate with zero oversight.

Three state bills (S447, H3155, H4013) would set some rules, but all three are stalled. None of them address what Flock does with data on its own servers.

I am asking the County Council to:
- Require the Sheriff's Office to disclose all 40 camera locations and data-sharing agreements
- Establish a county oversight policy with data retention limits, access controls, and regular public auditing
- Hold a public hearing before any renewal or expansion of the Flock contract

Hays County, TX cancelled its Flock contract after looking into the data-sharing practices. Newberry's 40 cameras are a lot of surveillance for a small community. Residents should at least know the terms.

Sincerely,
[Your name and address]

label: Newberry City Council
subject: The county has 40 cameras, and Newberry needs local oversight
body:
Dear [NAME],

The Newberry County Sheriff's Office runs 40 Flock Safety license plate reader cameras, one of the highest per-capita deployments in South Carolina. Anyone driving through the city of Newberry gets caught by this network. The data goes straight into SLED's statewide database: 422 million plate reads, three-year retention, accessible to over 2,000 users.

Flock Safety gave federal agencies direct access to local police cameras and did not tell the departments. Their CEO denied it in July 2025 and reversed course three weeks later. No city or county policy governs how this data gets used, kept, or audited. And once it enters the Flock network, there is no getting it back.

The Newberry City Council has the authority to set rules for its own jurisdiction even when the county has not. Other cities across the country have done exactly that.

Three state bills (S447, H3155, H4013) are stalled in committee. None of them touch what Flock does with data on its servers.

I am asking the City Council to:
- Request a briefing from the Sheriff's Office on how the 40-camera network affects Newberry residents
- Pass a city ordinance requiring council approval before any surveillance technology is deployed within city limits
- Establish a data retention limit and public use policy for any ALPR system the city may adopt in the future

Sedona, AZ cancelled its Flock contract after residents pushed back. Newberry should set rules before cameras arrive in city hands, not after.

Sincerely,
[Your name and address]

label: Greenwood County Council
subject: 65 cameras across three agencies, and nobody coordinating them
body:
Dear [NAME],

Greenwood County has about 65 Flock Safety cameras split across three agencies: the County Sheriff's Office, Greenwood City PD, and Lander University PD. Three agencies, three separate deployments, no county-level coordination. There is no transparency portal, no published data-sharing policy, and no council framework governing how this combined network works.

All of that data feeds into SLED's statewide database, which stores 422 million plate reads for three years and is accessible to over 2,000 users. Flock Safety secretly gave federal agencies direct access to local cameras nationwide. Their CEO denied it publicly in July 2025, then admitted it three weeks later. Once data hits the Flock network, no local agency can pull it out.

With three agencies running cameras independently, nobody tracks the total count, who can access the combined data, or whether cameras overlap. A resident could be captured by multiple systems on the same stretch of road and no one is coordinating how that information gets used.

Three state bills (S447, H3155, H4013) are stalled. None regulate what Flock does with the data on its own servers.

I am asking the County Council to:
- Require all three agencies to disclose their camera locations, costs, and data-sharing agreements to the council and the public
- Establish a unified county oversight policy covering data retention, access, and auditing
- Hold a public hearing before any agency in the county expands its ALPR deployment

Cambridge, MA put in place oversight rules for surveillance technology, including public reporting and elected body approval. Greenwood County's three-agency network needs, at minimum, a coordinated set of rules.

Sincerely,
[Your name and address]

label: Greenwood City Council
subject: Greenwood PD runs Flock cameras with no public rules
body:
Dear [NAME],

Greenwood PD operates Flock Safety license plate reader cameras as part of a county network totaling about 65 cameras across three agencies: the County Sheriff's Office, Greenwood City PD, and Lander University PD. Neither the county nor the city has an oversight policy. No transparency portal, no published data retention rules, no council framework for who can access the data or how it gets shared.

The data goes well beyond Greenwood. SLED stores 422 million plate reads for three years, accessible to over 2,000 users at 99+ agencies. Flock Safety gave federal agencies direct access to local cameras without telling police departments. Their CEO denied it publicly, then admitted it weeks later. Once data enters the network, Greenwood PD cannot pull it back.

The consequences of running cameras without rules are already showing up across the Upstate. In Greenville, police drew weapons on two sisters and handcuffed them after a Flock camera wrongly flagged their rental car as stolen. In Spartanburg, the county sheriff who oversaw surveillance camera deployments pled guilty to federal criminal charges. SCDOT's own Secretary found over 200 unpermitted Flock cameras on state roads.

Three state bills (S447, H3155, H4013) are stalled. None address Flock's own data practices. Until the state acts, local rules are the only check.

I am asking the City Council to:
- Require Greenwood PD to disclose all camera locations, costs, and data-sharing agreements
- Pass a city surveillance ordinance requiring council approval before deploying or expanding automated surveillance technology
- Establish data retention limits and mandatory access logging for ALPR data

Austin, TX ended its Flock contract over privacy and data-sharing concerns. Greenwood should set rules before the system grows any further.

Sincerely,
[Your name and address]

label: Chester County Council
subject: 25+ cameras in a county of 32,000, and no public say in it
body:
Dear [NAME],

Chester County has at least 25 Flock Safety cameras run by the County Sheriff's Office and Chester PD combined. For a county of about 32,000 people, that works out to roughly one camera per 1,280 residents, one of the higher ratios in the state. No public hearing was held. No county council vote set up an oversight framework.

All of this data feeds into SLED's statewide database: 422 million plate reads, three-year retention, over 2,000 users at 99+ agencies. Flock Safety gave federal agencies direct access to local cameras without telling the departments involved. Their CEO denied it in July 2025 and admitted it three weeks later.

Chester County sits on the I-77 corridor between Columbia and Charlotte. Every commuter, every through-traveler has their plate logged, stored for years, and searchable by agencies they have never heard of. No local policy governs any of it.

Three state bills (S447, H3155, H4013) are stalled in committee. None regulate what Flock does with data on its servers.

I am asking the County Council to:
- Require the Sheriff's Office and Chester PD to disclose all camera locations and data-sharing agreements
- Establish a county oversight policy with data retention limits, access controls, and regular auditing
- Hold a public hearing before any expansion of the ALPR network

Hays County, TX cancelled its Flock contract after learning how data sharing actually works. Chester County residents on the I-77 corridor deserve to know their movements are being tracked, and under what rules.

Sincerely,
[Your name and address]

label: Chester City Council
subject: Chester PD's cameras feed a network the city does not control
body:
Dear [NAME],

Chester PD operates Flock Safety license plate reader cameras as part of a county network of at least 25 cameras. The data does not stay local. It feeds into SLED's statewide database: 422 million plate reads stored for three years, accessible to over 2,000 users at 99+ agencies. Flock Safety gave federal agencies direct access to local cameras without telling departments. Their CEO lied about it publicly before admitting the truth weeks later.

Chester sits on the I-77 corridor between Columbia and Charlotte. Every commuter, every traveler, every resident driving through town has their plate captured by cameras the city council never formally authorized or set rules for. No published data retention policy, no transparency portal, no public oversight. The data collected on this corridor connects to surveillance networks running in both directions, linking South Carolina systems to those in North Carolina.

Flock's whole product is built around sharing data across jurisdictions. A camera in Chester captures a plate, and within seconds that data is searchable by agencies the city has no relationship with. Federal agencies do not need a warrant to query it. SCDOT's own Secretary found over 200 unpermitted Flock cameras on state roads. Three state bills (S447, H3155, H4013) that would set basic rules are stalled in committee, and none of them regulate what Flock does with data on its own servers.

I am asking the City Council to:
- Require Chester PD to disclose all camera locations, costs, and data-sharing agreements
- Pass a city ordinance setting data retention limits and requiring council approval before any surveillance technology is deployed or expanded
- Request a public briefing on how ALPR data collected in Chester is shared with outside agencies

Denver, CO requires elected body approval and public notice before new surveillance technology goes live. Chester is a small city on a major corridor. It should have oversight that matches the volume of data flowing through its cameras.

Sincerely,
[Your name and address]

label: Sumter County Council
subject: The House Speaker's law firm defends SLED's database, and Sumter County has no oversight of its own
body:
Dear [NAME],

The Sumter County Sheriff's Office operates 30 Flock Safety cameras. Sumter PD is listed as a data-sharing partner on the Flock network. All of this feeds into SLED's statewide database: 422 million plate reads, three-year retention, over 2,000 users. There is no county oversight policy.

Sumter has a particular connection to this issue. Smith Robinson, a Sumter-based law firm, is leading SLED's defense in the SCPIF v. SLED lawsuit challenging the legality of the state's license plate database. One of the firm's co-founding partners is Murrell Smith Jr., the Speaker of the South Carolina House. Three ALPR bills (S447, H3155, H4013) have stalled in the legislature he leads, while his firm defends SLED's authority to run the database without legislative authorization.

Regardless of how that lawsuit turns out, the county council can set local rules. Flock Safety gave federal agencies direct access to local cameras without telling departments, and their CEO denied it before admitting it weeks later. Shaw Air Force Base sits next to Sumter, and SLED's database has been accessed from military installations statewide.

I am asking the County Council to:
- Require the Sheriff's Office to disclose all 30 camera locations and data-sharing agreements
- Establish a county oversight policy with data retention limits, access controls, and regular auditing
- Hold a public hearing on ALPR use in Sumter County, including questions about federal and military access to local data

Santa Cruz, CA established strict oversight of surveillance technology with civilian review and public reporting. Sumter County sits at the intersection of state politics and federal military presence. Residents deserve local accountability.

Sincerely,
[Your name and address]

label: Sumter City Council
subject: Sumter PD shares data with a network no one locally oversees
body:
Dear [NAME],

Sumter PD is a Flock Safety data-sharing partner, which means the department can search camera data from agencies across the region and the country. The Sumter County Sheriff's Office runs 30 Flock cameras of its own. All of this data flows into SLED's statewide database: 422 million plate reads, three-year retention, accessible to over 2,000 users at 99+ agencies.

There is no city oversight framework. No published use policy. No transparency portal. The city council never voted to authorize these data-sharing arrangements or set rules for how the data is retained, audited, or accessed by outside agencies.

Flock Safety gave federal agencies direct access to local cameras without telling departments. Their CEO denied it in July 2025 and admitted it weeks later. Shaw Air Force Base, one of the largest Air Combat Command installations in the country, sits right next to Sumter. SLED's database has been accessed from military installations statewide. Whether military or federal law enforcement can access Sumter's camera data has no public answer, and no local policy requires one.

The Speaker of the South Carolina House, whose Sumter-based law firm is defending SLED in the lawsuit over the statewide database, has not moved any of the three ALPR bills (S447, H3155, H4013) through committee. Until the state acts, local rules are the only safeguard.

I am asking the City Council to:
- Require Sumter PD to disclose all data-sharing agreements and publish a public use policy for ALPR data
- Pass an ordinance requiring city council approval before deploying or expanding automated surveillance technology
- Request an accounting of which agencies, local, state, and federal, have accessed Sumter's camera data

Eugene, OR rejected a Flock proposal after community members demanded answers about data sharing. Sumter residents should get the chance to ask the same questions.

Sincerely,
[Your name and address]

label: Orangeburg County Council
subject: Two ALPR vendors, tiny-town police with Flock, and 1,000 campus cameras
body:
Dear [NAME],

Orangeburg County is running two competing ALPR vendors at the same time. The city's Department of Public Safety uses Flock Safety with 29 cameras. The County Sheriff's Office uses NDI Recognition Systems. Branchville (population 900), Cameron (population 400), and Elloree (population 600), three of the smallest municipalities in the state, also participate in Flock's national data-sharing network. On top of all that, Claflin University operates 4 Flock cameras plus 144 traditional surveillance cameras, with plans to add facial recognition.

Two vendors means residents' data flows into two separate commercial systems with different retention policies and sharing agreements. No county-level body coordinates how these overlapping systems work. All of it feeds into SLED's statewide database: 422 million plate reads, three-year retention.

Flock Safety gave federal agencies direct access to local cameras without telling the departments involved. Their CEO denied it publicly and admitted it weeks later. Orangeburg County's two HBCUs, SC State and Claflin, bring a younger, more transient population that lives under this surveillance infrastructure but has no electoral power to change it.

Three state bills (S447, H3155, H4013) are stalled. None regulate vendor data practices.

I am asking the County Council to:
- Conduct a county-wide audit of all ALPR and surveillance camera deployments, across all vendors, agencies, and institutions
- Establish a unified county oversight policy covering data retention, access controls, and public reporting
- Hold a public hearing with input from university communities and small-town residents affected by the surveillance network

Cambridge, MA requires public reporting and elected body approval for all surveillance technology. Orangeburg County's patchwork of vendors and agencies needs, at minimum, a single set of rules.

Sincerely,
[Your name and address]

label: Orangeburg City Council
subject: 29 Flock cameras, 144 campus cameras, and no city oversight
body:
Dear [NAME],

The Orangeburg Department of Public Safety operates 29 Flock Safety cameras. Claflin University runs 4 more, plus 144 traditional surveillance cameras, with plans to reach 200 and add facial recognition at a cost of $300,000. SC State has planned 700 cameras of its own. The city and its campuses are blanketed in surveillance, and there is no city council oversight framework for any of it.

Each shooting near the campuses has sped up camera deployment. The August 2022 Buckley Street shooting, the January 2024 Claflin science building incident, the February 2026 SC State residential complex shooting. After each one, more cameras, less debate. But cameras have not stopped the violence. They have expanded a system that tracks everyone, including over 4,000 students who cannot vote in Orangeburg city elections.

Flock Safety gave federal agencies direct access to local cameras without telling departments. Their CEO denied it in July 2025 and reversed course weeks later. All data feeds into SLED's 422-million-read database. Orangeburg's poverty rate is 23.9%, well above the state average.

Three state bills (S447, H3155, H4013) are stalled. None regulate vendor data practices.

I am asking the City Council to:
- Require the Department of Public Safety to publish camera locations, data-sharing agreements, and annual access reports
- Pass a city surveillance ordinance requiring council approval before expanding ALPR or adding facial recognition
- Include university and community representatives in a public hearing on surveillance oversight

Austin, TX ended its Flock contract after reviewing data-sharing concerns. Orangeburg's cameras have not stopped the violence, but they are tracking every resident and student. The city should at least set the rules.

Sincerely,
[Your name and address]

label: Union County Council
subject: 23 cameras for 26,700 people, one of the highest ratios in SC
body:
Dear [NAME],

The Union County Sheriff's Office operates 23 Flock Safety license plate reader cameras for a county of about 26,700 people. That works out to roughly one camera for every 1,160 residents, a higher per-capita rate than Greenville PD's 57 cameras covering 72,000 people. For a county with a median household income of $41,200 and a 17.9% family poverty rate, that is a lot of money going to surveillance. No public hearing was held. No county council vote authorized an oversight framework.

All 23 cameras feed into SLED's statewide database: 422 million plate reads, three-year retention, over 2,000 users at 99+ agencies. Flock Safety gave federal agencies direct access to local cameras without telling departments. Their CEO denied it in July 2025 and admitted it three weeks later. Once data enters the network, Union County cannot control who searches it.

Union County has no interstate, no major university, and a declining population. Yet it has one of the densest ALPR deployments in the state relative to its size. Every driver's movements are logged, stored, and shared across a national network regardless of county size.

Three state bills (S447, H3155, H4013) are stalled. None regulate Flock's data practices.

I am asking the County Council to:
- Require the Sheriff's Office to disclose all 23 camera locations, costs, and data-sharing agreements
- Establish a county oversight policy with data retention limits and mandatory access auditing
- Hold a public hearing so residents can weigh in on whether this level of surveillance is appropriate for the community

Sedona, AZ, a town of 10,000, cancelled its Flock contract after reviewing data-sharing practices. Union County is not too small for oversight. It may be too small to afford surveillance without it.

Sincerely,
[Your name and address]

label: Union City Council
subject: The county has 23 cameras tracking Union residents, and the city should set its own rules
body:
Dear [NAME],

The Union County Sheriff's Office operates 23 Flock Safety cameras, one of the highest per-capita ALPR deployments in South Carolina for a county of 26,700. Union city residents are captured by this network every time they drive. The data feeds into SLED's 422-million-read statewide database with three-year retention, accessible to over 2,000 users at 99+ agencies.

Flock Safety gave federal agencies direct access to local cameras without telling departments. Their CEO denied it publicly and admitted it weeks later. There is no city or county oversight framework, no transparency portal, no public use policy.

The county council has not acted. But Union city has the authority to set rules for its own jurisdiction. If the city ever adopts its own ALPR system, or if county cameras already cover city streets, there should be a policy in place before that happens, not after.

Three state bills (S447, H3155, H4013) are stalled. None regulate what Flock does with data on its servers.

I am asking the City Council to:
- Request a briefing from the county on how the 23-camera network affects Union city residents
- Pass a city ordinance requiring council approval before any automated surveillance technology is deployed within city limits
- Establish a public use policy with data retention limits should the city ever operate its own cameras

Hays County, TX cancelled its Flock contract after examining data-sharing practices. Union is a small community, but the surveillance network it feeds into is not. The city should have rules.

Sincerely,
[Your name and address]

label: Oconee County Council
subject: The Sheriff's Association president runs Oconee's cameras, but the council should run the oversight
body:
Dear [NAME],

Oconee County Sheriff Mike Crenshaw operates 6 Flock Safety cameras. He is also President of the South Carolina Sheriffs' Association and testified before the state Senate in favor of ALPR technology, pointing to a 2025 kidnapping case where Flock cameras helped recover a 9-year-old girl in Edgefield County. That was a real success.

But success stories do not eliminate the need for oversight. Flock Safety gave federal agencies direct access to local cameras without telling departments. Their CEO denied it publicly and admitted it weeks later. SLED stores 422 million plate reads for three years. Oconee County sits on the I-85 corridor at the Georgia border, a drug interdiction chokepoint where the DEA, CBP, and HSI all operate. Whether those agencies can access Oconee's camera data has no public answer.

Seneca PD runs 4 more Flock cameras. Pendleton PD is on the Flock data-sharing network. No public hearing has been held. No county oversight policy exists.

Three state bills (S447, H3155, H4013) are stalled. None regulate what Flock does with data on its servers.

I am asking the County Council to:
- Require the Sheriff's Office and Seneca PD to disclose all camera locations and data-sharing agreements
- Establish a county oversight policy with data retention limits and mandatory access auditing
- Request an accounting of which agencies, including federal, have accessed Oconee County's ALPR data

Denver, CO requires public notice and elected body approval before surveillance technology is deployed. Sheriff Crenshaw's cameras may do good work, but the council, not the sheriff, should set the rules.

Sincerely,
[Your name and address]

label: Walhalla City Council
subject: Oconee County has cameras on I-85, and Walhalla should set rules before they reach city limits
body:
Dear [NAME],

Oconee County Sheriff Mike Crenshaw operates 6 Flock Safety cameras. Seneca PD runs 4 more. Pendleton PD is on the Flock data-sharing network. Walhalla does not currently run its own ALPR system, but the county's cameras track vehicles throughout the area and the data feeds into SLED's statewide database: 422 million plate reads, three-year retention.

Sheriff Crenshaw, who is also President of the South Carolina Sheriffs' Association, has been a vocal advocate for the technology. But advocacy is not oversight. Flock Safety gave federal agencies direct access to local cameras without telling departments. Their CEO denied it in July 2025 and admitted it weeks later. Oconee County sits on the I-85 corridor at the Georgia border, where DEA and other federal agencies run drug interdiction operations.

Walhalla has a chance to act before cameras arrive in city hands. Setting rules now is simpler than unwinding a system later.

Three state bills (S447, H3155, H4013) are stalled. None regulate Flock's data practices.

I am asking the City Council to:
- Pass a city ordinance requiring council approval and public notice before any automated surveillance technology is deployed within city limits
- Request a briefing from the county on how the existing camera network affects Walhalla residents
- Establish a public use policy with data retention limits for any future ALPR system

Sedona, AZ, a small community not unlike Walhalla, cancelled its Flock contract after residents raised concerns. It is easier to build oversight into a system before it exists than to add it on after.

Sincerely,
[Your name and address]

label: Kershaw County Council
subject: 22 Flock cameras track Kershaw County roads with no oversight policy
body:
Dear [NAME],

Kershaw County has 22 Flock Safety license plate reader cameras across two agencies: 10 through the Sheriff's Office and 12 through Camden PD. Every plate read goes into a national network and into SLED's statewide database, which stores 422 million reads for three years and is accessible to over 2,000 users at 99+ agencies.

No county-level policy governs how this data is collected, shared, or audited. Neither agency has a public transparency portal, and there is no record of a public hearing before the cameras went up.

In neighboring Richland County, the Sheriff's Department quoted a journalist $9,152 just to produce two CSV files showing how Flock cameras are used and who accesses the data. Florence PD handed over the same records for free. That kind of stonewalling becomes possible when oversight policies do not exist. Kershaw County has none.

Flock's CEO denied having federal contracts in July 2025 and admitted it three weeks later. The company had secretly given Customs and Border Protection direct access to local police cameras nationwide. Once data enters the Flock network, local agencies cannot control who searches it. The I-20 corridor connecting Camden to Columbia means vehicles passing between Kershaw and Richland counties hit overlapping ALPR coverage with no coordinated rules.

Three state bills (S447, H3155, H4013) would set rules for police use of camera data, but none regulate what Flock itself does with the data on its servers. Until the state acts, county oversight is the only check.

I am asking the County Council to:
- Require the Sheriff's Office and Camden PD to disclose all Flock camera locations and data-sharing agreements
- Establish a county oversight policy covering data retention, access controls, and regular auditing
- Hold a public hearing before any renewal or expansion of surveillance contracts

Sincerely,
[Your name and address]

label: Lexington County Council
subject: Three Lexington County agencies run Flock cameras with no county oversight
body:
Dear [NAME],

At least three law enforcement agencies in Lexington County operate Flock Safety license plate reader cameras: Lexington PD (4 confirmed cameras), Cayce PD, and Chapin PD. Each department independently shares data through Flock's national network, and all plate reads flow into SLED's statewide database of 422 million reads with three-year retention.

Cayce PD sits directly across the Congaree River from Columbia, where the surveillance footprints of the two counties effectively merge. Chapin PD, with only 8 sworn officers, uses the Flock network as a force multiplier, pulling plate reads from agencies across the state and beyond. No county-level policy coordinates these overlapping deployments or sets rules for how the data gets accessed and shared.

In neighboring Richland County, the Sheriff's Department quoted $9,152 for two CSV files about Flock camera usage. That same agency, which stonewalls public records requests, has access to the same data network Lexington County agencies feed into. Flock's CEO denied federal contracts in July 2025 and admitted it three weeks later. The company had given Customs and Border Protection access to local cameras nationwide without telling departments.

Three state bills (S447, H3155, H4013) would set rules for police use of camera data, but none regulate what Flock itself does with the data on its servers. Rep. Todd Rutherford has been the most persistent voice for ALPR regulation in the legislature. Until a bill passes, county oversight is the only safeguard.

I am asking the County Council to:
- Require all agencies operating Flock cameras in Lexington County to disclose their deployments and data-sharing agreements
- Establish a unified county oversight policy covering data retention, access, and auditing
- Hold a public hearing before any expansion of the camera network

Sincerely,
[Your name and address]

label: Lexington City Council
subject: Lexington PD's Flock cameras need local oversight to match the transparency portal
body:
Dear [NAME],

Lexington PD operates 4 Flock Safety license plate reader cameras and is one of the few small-town departments in the Midlands with a published Flock transparency portal. That is a good start. But a transparency portal is not the same as oversight. The portal describes Flock's standard policies: 30-day data retention, no immigration enforcement, all access logged. What it does not show is who has actually accessed the data, how many outside agencies have queried Lexington's cameras, or what happens to the data once it enters the national network.

Flock Safety's CEO denied having federal contracts in July 2025 and admitted it three weeks later. The company had given Customs and Border Protection direct access to local police cameras across the country without telling departments. Once data enters the Flock network, Lexington PD cannot control who searches it. SLED's statewide database stores 422 million plate reads with three-year retention, accessible to over 2,000 users.

In neighboring Richland County, the Sheriff quoted $9,152 to release two CSV files about Flock camera usage. That is what happens when surveillance technology runs without real local oversight. Lexington has a chance to set a better example.

Three state bills (S447, H3155, H4013) are stalled in committee. None regulate what Flock does with data on its own servers. Until the state acts, local action is the only check.

I am asking the City Council to:
- Require Lexington PD to publish annual reports detailing data access, outside queries, and sharing agreements
- Pass an ordinance requiring council approval before any new surveillance technology is deployed or expanded
- Hold a public hearing on the Flock contract terms and data-sharing practices

Sincerely,
[Your name and address]

label: Local Representative
subject: Our community needs oversight of surveillance cameras
body:
Dear [NAME],

More than 1,000 Flock Safety license plate reader cameras are operating across South Carolina. No city or county in the state has a public oversight policy for this technology. The legislature has never addressed ALPR data privacy.

Flock secretly gave federal agencies direct access to local police camera networks across the country without telling the departments involved. Their CEO denied it on camera in July 2025 and admitted it weeks later. Once location data enters Flock's network, local agencies can't get it back. Federal agencies don't need a warrant to search it.

In Greenville, two sisters were handcuffed at gunpoint after a Flock camera wrongly flagged their rental car as stolen. In Spartanburg, the county sheriff who oversaw surveillance deployments pled guilty to federal criminal charges. SLED keeps license plate data for three years with no public accountability.

Three state bills (S447, H3155, H4013) would set rules for police use of camera data, but none regulate what Flock itself does with it on its own servers. Until the state closes that gap, local oversight is the only check on this system.

Communities elsewhere have looked at how Flock works and acted. Hays County, TX and Sedona, AZ cancelled their contracts. Cambridge, MA established strict oversight rules. South Carolina has done neither.

I'm asking you to:
- Require disclosure of any surveillance camera deployments and data-sharing agreements in our community
- Hold a public hearing on surveillance technology oversight
- Establish a policy requiring elected body approval before new automated surveillance systems are deployed

Residents deserve to know who is tracking their movements and under what rules.

Sincerely,
[Your name and address]
